title: Recursive functions

sources.claim: A recursive Python function solves a smaller instance, reaches an explicit base case, and combines returned results while consuming call-stack depth.

canonicalExplanation.summary: A recursive Python function solves a smaller instance, reaches an explicit base case, and combines returned results while consuming call-stack depth.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A factorial function rejects negative input, returns 1 at zero, and recurs with n - 1 so every valid call moves toward the base case. Your goal is to write, trace and test a recursive routine with explicit base and progress cases. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Core collections and text; pf abstraction calls; Function contracts and returns; pf abstraction recursion. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language-specific operational model
sections.body: A recursive Python function solves a smaller instance, reaches an explicit base case, and combines returned results while consuming call-stack depth. This model is applied through the declared Python: Beginner to Intermediate behavior rather than a language-neutral slogan.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure model
sections.body: Python does not guarantee tail-call elimination, recursion depth is limited, cycles need visited state, exponential recurrence can repeat work, and iterative forms may be clearer. The learner must reject this documented misconception: Any function that calls itself will eventually stop, and tail recursion uses constant stack space in Python.

includes: Implement terminating recursion and compare it with iterative alternatives under Python's call limits.
Write, trace and test a recursive routine with explicit base and progress cases.
excludes: Python does not guarantee tail-call elimination, recursion depth is limited, cycles need visited state, exponential recurrence can repeat work, and iterative forms may be clearer.

outcomes: Write, trace and test a recursive routine with explicit base and progress cases.

examples.title: Recursive functions: direct language case
examples.situation: A factorial function rejects negative input, returns 1 at zero, and recurs with n - 1 so every valid call moves toward the base case.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the concrete Python: Beginner to Intermediate state or tool result: A factorial function rejects negative input, returns 1 at zero, and recurs with n - 1 so every valid call moves toward the base case.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the result with this correction: State the base case and a strictly smaller progress measure, and choose iteration when depth can exceed Python's recursion limit.
examples.result: The example meets the atomic outcome because State the base case and a strictly smaller progress measure, and choose iteration when depth can exceed Python's recursion limit.

examples.title: Recursive functions: boundary and transfer case
examples.situation: A deep linked structure is traversed iteratively to avoid recursion limits, while a small tree exercise keeps recursion because its shape matches the definition.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name what changed from the direct case: A deep linked structure is traversed iteratively to avoid recursion limits, while a small tree exercise keeps recursion because its shape matches the definition.

Step 2 — Keep the language boundary explicit: Python does not guarantee tail-call elimination, recursion depth is limited, cycles need visited state, exponential recurrence can repeat work, and iterative forms may be clearer.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting but incorrect rule: Any function that calls itself will eventually stop, and tail recursion uses constant stack space in Python.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Write, trace and test a recursive routine with explicit base and progress cases.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Any function that calls itself will eventually stop, and tail recursion uses constant stack space in Python.
misconceptions.correction: State the base case and a strictly smaller progress measure, and choose iteration when depth can exceed Python's recursion limit.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Predict both examples and identify the first observable contradiction in this belief: Any function that calls itself will eventually stop, and tail recursion uses constant stack space in Python.

analogy.example: a callable service counter with a published request form, binding rules, retained enclosing notes, and a recursive delegation limit can introduce Recursive functions, after the canonical Python: Beginner to Intermediate rule is stated first.
limitations: definition-time defaults, LEGB lookup, late binding, exceptions, and stack limits have no exact service-counter counterpart
